---

description: >
  Write a decision memo for weekly resets: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "weekly resets decision-memo", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-decision-memo.
metadata:

---
# /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for weekly resets: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "weekly resets decision-memo", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Mid-reset, the user hits a genuine conflict: two projects both "need" this week, a side project competes with recovery time, or a standing commitment collides with a one-off opportunity. The plan can't be finished until someone decides where the week's scarce discretionary hours go. Use this to make that call explicitly instead of letting the loudest deadline win by default.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The competing claims on the week: each contender, its deadline, and what happens if it slips seven days
- Discretionary hours actually available this week after the fixed calendar
- What the user said mattered this month or quarter (goals, promises made to others)
- Which contenders are commitments to other people versus commitments only to self
- What was chosen last time this conflict came up, and how that went

## Workflow

1. State the decision in one sentence: "This week's [N] discretionary hours go primarily to ___." If it can't be phrased that way, the conflict isn't yet clear enough to decide.
2. List the contenders with three facts each: hours needed, hard deadline (real or self-imposed - label which), and the concrete consequence of deferring one week.
3. Run the capacity check: if the top two contenders together exceed available hours, splitting the week between them means both slip. Say so plainly.
4. Score against the month, not the moment. The contender that advances a stated monthly goal or a promise to another person outranks the one that is merely urgent-feeling.
5. Recommend one primary claim on the week, and name what gets explicitly deferred - including the sentence the user can send to anyone affected by the deferral.
6. Note reversibility: most weekly allocations are two-way doors. If the choice turns out wrong by Wednesday, what is the swap cost? Low swap cost is a reason to decide fast, not to agonize.

## Output format

**Decision** - one sentence: where this week's discretionary hours go

**Contenders**

| Option | Hours needed | Deadline (real or self-imposed) | Consequence of a 1-week slip |

**Recommendation** - the primary claim, with the two strongest reasons

**Explicitly deferred** - what loses this week, plus a ready-to-send sentence for anyone affected

**Revisit trigger** - the mid-week condition that would reverse this call

## Do

- Distinguish real deadlines from self-imposed ones - self-imposed deadlines are inputs, not trump cards
- Weigh promises to other people above self-only ambitions when hours are short; broken external promises compound
- Keep the memo to something readable in two minutes - it feeds a weekly plan, not a board meeting
- Recommend one winner; a memo that recommends "balance" has decided nothing

## Don't

- Don't let recency decide - the email that arrived an hour ago is not more important because it is newer
- Don't split scarce hours evenly across all contenders to avoid the discomfort of choosing; that is how everything slips together
- Don't treat a weekly allocation as a life decision - it resets in seven days, so decide and move
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The decision fits in one sentence and names a single primary claim on the week
- [ ] Every contender shows the concrete cost of a one-week slip, not vague urgency
- [ ] The deferred items are named, with a message the user can actually send
- [ ] Hours needed by the recommendation fit inside hours available
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-decision-memo or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for weekly resets: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "weekly resets operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for weekly resets: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "weekly resets operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Sunday evening (or Friday afternoon) when the coming week is a fog: commitments scattered across email, texts, a calendar, and memory; last week's plan died by Tuesday; the same three tasks keep getting carried forward. Use this to run a weekly reset that produces a week the user can actually execute - not an aspirational one.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The next 7 days of calendar: meetings, appointments, travel, school runs - the hard landscape
- Every open loop: task lists, flagged emails, sticky notes, mental nags
- Carry-overs from last week and *why* they slipped (no time, no energy, wrong task, avoidance)
- Real available hours after meetings, commute, meals, and family time - not the fantasy number
- Energy pattern: which days and times are reliably good for focused work
- The one outcome that would make this week a win

## Workflow

1. Capture before you triage. Sweep every source (inbox, notes app, texts, memory) into one list. Do not evaluate anything while capturing - mixing the two stalls both.
2. Lay the hard landscape first. Put fixed appointments, deadlines, and family commitments on the calendar before allocating any discretionary time.
3. Do the capacity math. Count actual free hours per day after meetings and life overhead. If the list needs 30 hours and the week holds 12, cut now on paper - not Thursday in a panic.
4. Pick one priority per day. One meaningful outcome each day, matched to the day's energy: deep work on the clear-morning day, errands and admin on the fragmented day.
5. Block the daily priority on the calendar first, then fit smaller tasks around it. Leave roughly 20% of each day unscheduled as buffer for the week's inevitable surprises.
6. Name what you're not doing. Move cut items to a "not this week" list so they stop leaking attention.
7. Set a 10-minute mid-week checkpoint (Wednesday works) to re-plan the back half instead of watching the plan rot.
8. Write down Monday's first action before finishing. A week that starts with "figure out what to do first" loses the morning.

## Output format

**Week of [date] - one-line intent**

**Hard landscape** - fixed commitments listed by day

**Daily plan**

| Day | One priority | Focus block | Other tasks | Buffer |

**Not this week** - deliberately deferred items

**Carry-over watchlist** - anything that has slipped twice, each with a decision: do, delegate, shrink, or delete

**Mid-week checkpoint** - day, time, and what to re-check

**Monday's first action**

## Do

- Subtract meetings and life overhead before committing focus work - plan the hours that actually exist
- Keep one capture list; a plan assembled from three task apps is already broken
- Keep the reset itself to 30-45 minutes at the same time weekly - the cadence matters more than the format
- Force a decision on anything that has slipped three weeks running: shrink it, schedule it, or delete it

## Don't

- Don't schedule every hour; a plan with no slack breaks on the first surprise and takes the week's credibility with it
- Don't redesign the planning system during the reset - tinkering with the format is the most respectable form of procrastination
- Don't copy someone else's weekly template wholesale; borrow one element at a time and keep only what survives a month
- Don't copy external templates or claim live calendar or task-app access without a successful connector/tool call - work from what the user provides
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions such as sending, sharing, or modifying anything on the user's behalf

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Committed focus hours are less than or equal to actual free hours after the hard landscape
- [ ] Exactly one named priority per day - not three
- [ ] Something was explicitly cut or deferred; a plan that cuts nothing ignored capacity
- [ ] Every day has buffer and the mid-week checkpoint is scheduled
- [ ] Monday's first action is concrete enough to start without thinking
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-risk-review
description: >
  Review operational risk for weekly resets: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "weekly resets risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-risk-review

Review operational risk for weekly resets: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "weekly resets risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A weekly plan exists - and the user has watched versions of it collapse before. Use this as a five-minute pre-mortem on the drafted week: find the days it will break, decide in advance what gets sacrificed when it does, and set the early-warning signs. Best run immediately after the reset, while the plan can still be adjusted for free.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The drafted weekly plan (priorities, focus blocks, hard landscape)
- Which tasks depend on other people responding, delivering, or showing up
- The week's known pressure points: back-to-back days, travel, evening events, a partner's crunch week
- How the last two plans actually failed (interruptions, energy crash, scope creep, a surprise obligation)
- Anything with a hard external deadline this week where slipping has real cost

## Workflow

1. Walk the plan day by day and flag structural weak points: days with zero buffer, focus blocks butted against meetings, two big outcomes sharing one day.
2. List external dependencies - every task that stalls if someone else is late. For each, note the last responsible moment to chase them.
3. Check the energy assumption: if Thursday's plan requires deep focus but Wednesday ends with a late event, the plan is betting against biology. Flag it.
4. Rate each risk for likelihood and impact using the user's own history - the failure mode that killed the last two weeks is "high likelihood" no matter how confident this week feels.
5. Write one mitigation per top risk that changes the plan now (move the block, add buffer, send the chase-up email today), not a vow to "be more disciplined."
6. Define the drop order: when the week compresses - and it will - which items get sacrificed first, second, third. Deciding this while calm prevents the panic-triage that drops the wrong thing.
7. Set the tripwire: the observable signal (e.g., "two priorities missed by Wednesday noon") that triggers invoking the drop order rather than pushing harder.

## Output format

**Plan under review** - week of [date], one-line summary

**Risk table**

| Risk | Day exposed | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation (a change made now) |

**External dependencies** - who, what, and the date to chase by

**Drop order** - items ranked by what gets cut first when time compresses

**Tripwire** - the observable signal that triggers the drop order

## Do

- Weight risks by the user's actual track record, not by how the week feels on Sunday
- Prefer mitigations that alter the calendar today over resolutions about future behavior
- Protect the one non-negotiable outcome of the week with buffer on both sides
- Keep the review to five minutes of reading - it guards a week, not a product launch

## Don't

- Don't list ten risks with equal weight; three risks with real mitigations beat a comprehensive-looking table nobody acts on
- Don't propose "wake up earlier" or "just focus harder" as mitigation - plans that require a better version of the user this week will fail like the last ones
- Don't let the review become an excuse to rebuild the whole plan; it adjusts, it doesn't restart
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The top risk matches how recent weeks actually failed
- [ ] Every mitigation is a concrete change the user can make today, on the calendar or in a message
- [ ] The drop order is explicit and ranked - no ties
- [ ] The tripwire is observable (a missed thing by a named time), not a feeling
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

# /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for weekly resets: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "weekly resets stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The weekly plan is set, but it only works if the people around the user cooperate: a partner who needs to know Tuesday evening is spoken for, a manager who should hear that the report lands Thursday not Wednesday, a co-parent covering Thursday pickup. Use this to turn the plan into short, per-person messages - because a plan nobody else knows about gets overwritten by the first person who asks for something.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The finished weekly plan (priorities, focus blocks, unavailable windows)
- Who is affected: partner, manager, co-parent, teammates, anyone sharing logistics
- What each person needs *from* the user this week, and what the user needs from them
- Any change from the normal pattern (working late Wednesday, offline Friday morning)
- Preferred channel and tone per person - a manager update and a fridge note are different artifacts

## Workflow

1. List every person whose week touches this plan, and sort them into two groups: people who need to *act* (cover a pickup, review a draft) and people who just need to *know* (don't book me Tuesday night).
2. For each person, extract only what concerns them. The manager doesn't need the gym schedule; the partner doesn't need the sprint priorities. Over-sharing buries the ask.
3. Lead every message with the ask or the headline change, not the backstory. "Can you take Thursday pickup?" first; reasons second, and only if needed.
4. Make asks answerable with yes/no plus a specific time: "Can you review the draft before Wednesday 2pm?" - not "when you get a chance."
5. Flag deviations from routine explicitly. People forgive changed plans they were told about; they resent ones they discovered.
6. Match the artifact to the audience: two-line text for the partner, three-bullet update for the manager, shared-calendar note for the household. Write each ready to send.
7. Note which asks are blocking - if the answer is no, the weekly plan changes - so the user sends those first and chases them by a named time.

## Output format

**Week of [date] - who needs to hear what**

**Per-person messages** (each ready to send)

- **[Name / role]** - channel · blocking or FYI
  - The message, in that person's register

**Blocking asks** - the yes/no answers the plan depends on, and by when

**Not shared** - anything deliberately kept private, so the user doesn't overshare by accident

## Do

- Write each message in the register of the relationship - texts to a partner shouldn't read like status reports
- Put the ask in the first sentence and a deadline on every request
- Keep it to the two to five people who genuinely need to hear; a broadcast to everyone is a message to no one
- Tell people about routine changes before they collide with them

## Don't

- Don't justify the whole productivity system to anyone - share the schedule change, not the philosophy behind it
- Don't disguise asks as updates; "just so you know I'm slammed Thursday" doesn't secure the pickup
- Don't promise buffer time away - protecting slack is the point of having it
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send anything on the user's behalf without explicit approval - messages to real people are a sensitive action

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every message is short enough to read in under 30 seconds and ready to paste into its channel
- [ ] Each ask is yes/no answerable with a named deadline
- [ ] Blocking asks are separated from FYIs
- [ ] Tone matches each relationship - no corporate voice aimed at family
- [ ] Nothing gets sent without the user's approval; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-weekly-reset-stakeholder-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-decision-memo
description: >
  Write a decision memo for priority filters: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "priority filters decision-memo", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-decision-memo.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for priority filters: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "priority filters decision-memo", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Too many commitments are competing for the same finite weeks and the user keeps deferring the choice, which is itself a choice - usually the worst one, since everything then slips a little. Use this when the situation needs a ruling: which of the five things happens, which waits, which dies. The memo's job is to make the tradeoff explicit and survivable - every "yes" in a full life is funded by a named "no," and this document names it instead of letting the calendar name it later.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The full contender list - including the obligations the user is embarrassed to consider dropping
- Real available capacity: hours per week actually free after the calendar's fixed commitments are subtracted
- For each contender: the concrete consequence of not doing it, and who bears that consequence
- Which deadlines are externally enforced vs. self-imposed (they are not the same species)
- Reversibility: what can be paused and resumed later vs. what dies if paused

## Workflow

1. Force every contender through the same two questions: "what actually happens if this doesn't happen this month?" and "who bears that?" Write the answers plainly - most lists shrink here, because half the items' honest consequence is "mild disappointment, mine, briefly." Vague dread is not a consequence.
2. Compute the budget before ranking: real weekly free hours times the weeks in scope, minus a slack reserve of roughly a fifth - surprises are a certainty, not a risk. The contenders' summed estimates against this number is the memo's central fact; if demand is double supply, no prioritization framework fixes it, only removal does.
3. Separate the deadline types: externally enforced deadlines (visa dates, filings, other people's dependencies) are constraints and get scheduled first; self-imposed deadlines are preferences and compete on merit. Treating a self-imposed date as sacred while a real one starves is the most common self-inflicted crisis.
4. Rank what remains by consequence-weight per hour, not by guilt, recency, or who asked loudest - and check reversibility as the tiebreak: between two similar items, defer the one that survives deferral. A paused hobby resumes; a missed enrollment window doesn't.
5. Write the ruling with the funding source named: "do A and B now; C waits until March and here is the calendar note that resurrects it; D is dropped and [person] gets told this week; E shrinks to the minimal version." Every deferral gets a revival date or it's a drop wearing a costume - say which it is.
6. State the cost of the recommendation honestly - what gets worse, who will be disappointed, what the user gives up - and the tripwire for revisiting: the one changed circumstance (deadline moves, project cancels, capacity jumps) that would legitimately reopen this decision. Absent the tripwire, the ruling stands; re-deciding weekly is how nothing ships.

## Output format

**The ruling** - do now / defer (with revival date) / drop (with who gets told) / shrink (to what), one line each

**Capacity arithmetic** - supply vs. demand, slack reserve shown

**Consequence table** - contender / what happens if skipped / who bears it / externally enforced?

**Why this ranking** - two or three sentences on the deciding logic

**Cost of the decision** - what gets worse and who's disappointed, stated plainly

**Notifications owed** - each person affected, told what, by when

**Revisit tripwire** - the specific event that reopens the decision; nothing else does

## Do

- Make every deferral carry a revival date and every drop carry a notification
- Schedule externally enforced deadlines before ranking anything discretionary
- Use reversibility as the tiebreak between near-equal contenders
- Keep a slack reserve out of the arithmetic - a full plan is a broken plan with better formatting

## Don't

- Don't rank by guilt or by who asked most recently; consequence per hour is the axis
- Don't produce a ranking that ignores the arithmetic - a beautiful top-ten against twelve free hours is fiction
- Don't disguise drops as deferrals; the undated "later" list is where commitments go to rot visibly
- Don't reopen the ruling without the tripwire firing - relitigating priorities weekly is the failure mode this memo exists to end
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't notify affected people or cancel anything on the user's behalf without explicit approval per action

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every contender has a written consequence-of-skipping and a bearer
- [ ] The supply/demand arithmetic appears with the slack reserve
- [ ] External and self-imposed deadlines are treated as different species
- [ ] Every deferral has a revival date; every drop has a notification plan
- [ ] The decision's cost is stated, not softened
- [ ] A single revisit tripwire is defined
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-decision-memo or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for priority filters: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "priority filters operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for priority filters: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "priority filters operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Everything feels urgent, the to-do list has three homes plus a head, and the week keeps getting decided by whoever pings loudest. Use this to install a standing prioritization operation: one capture point, a weekly triage with honest capacity math, and a daily pick that takes five minutes because the weekly session did the thinking. This is a system to run, not a philosophy to admire - it survives on the weeks the user is too busy for it, which is exactly when it earns its keep.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Every place commitments currently live: apps, notebooks, starred emails, chat promises, the mental list
- A real week's calendar - meetings, commute, family obligations, the recurring immovables
- The current candidate list of tasks and projects, unfiltered, guilt items included
- Deadlines that are actually externally enforced vs. self-imposed
- Who is affected when something gets dropped or deferred (boss, partner, clients) and how they'd hear about it

## Workflow

1. Establish the single capture point first: one inbox (one app list or one notebook page) where every new commitment lands the moment it appears - the hallway promise, the school email, the shower idea. Multiple lists are how commitments become invisible; the rule is capture everywhere, store in exactly one place.
2. Run the capacity math before choosing anything: take the nominal work hours, subtract meetings, commute, meals, admin, and a ~20% overhead for the day's inevitable friction - the remainder is the real discretionary capacity, usually a shock (a "40-hour week" commonly yields 10-15 usable hours). Priorities chosen without this number are wishes.
3. Lay the hard landscape into the calendar before any task planning: appointments, deadlines with external teeth, obligations to other people. Only the space that remains is available for chosen work - planning tasks before fixed commitments is how Tuesday gets triple-booked.
4. Triage the full candidate list against the real capacity: pick at most three must-happen items for the week (things with consequences if missed), a short should list sized to the remaining hours, and move everything else to a later list without guilt - it's parked, not deleted. If the musts alone exceed capacity, the plan's first action is renegotiation, not optimism.
5. Install the weekly session as the keystone: same day and time, 30-45 minutes, standing agenda - empty the capture inbox to zero, review what carried over (three-time carryovers get renegotiated, shrunk, or dropped, never re-carried), redo the capacity math for the coming week, pick the new musts. Everything else in the system can wobble; this session cannot.
6. Define the daily pick: each morning (or evening before), five minutes - choose today's 1-3 tasks from the weekly musts, matched to the day's actual open slots. No re-litigating the week daily; the weekly session decided, the daily pick just schedules.
7. Set the interruption rule for mid-week arrivals: new requests go to the capture inbox by default; the only queue-jump test is "does this have worse consequences than my current musts if it waits a week?" If a genuine emergency displaces a must, the displaced item's stakeholder gets told the same day - silent slippage is how trust erodes.

## Output format

**The system on one page** - capture point, weekly session slot, daily pick time

**Capacity math** - nominal hours → subtractions itemized → real weekly capacity

**This week's triage** - musts (max 3, with consequence stated) / shoulds (sized to hours) / later list / dropped-renegotiated

**Weekly session agenda** - the standing 5-line agenda with day and time

**Interruption rule** - the queue-jump test, verbatim, plus the same-day notification rule

**Renegotiation notes** - anything over capacity: who gets told what, by when

## Do

- Keep exactly one capture point and let everything land there before judgment
- Do the subtraction honestly - capacity math with meetings left in produces fantasy weeks
- Cap musts at three; a week with seven priorities has none
- Treat the weekly session as the load-bearing habit and protect it like a client meeting

## Don't

- Don't reorganize the system when a week goes badly - tinkering with tools is procrastination wearing a productivity costume; run the same system again
- Don't fill 100% of computed capacity; plans with no slack shatter on the first surprise
- Don't let three-time carryovers ride a fourth week - renegotiate, shrink, or drop them out loud
- Don't track more than the system needs to run; metrics about the system are not progress on the work
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send renegotiation messages or decline commitments on the user's behalf without explicit approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] There is exactly one capture point, named
- [ ] Capacity is a computed number with the subtractions shown
- [ ] Musts number three or fewer and each states its miss-consequence
- [ ] The weekly session has a day, a time, and a standing agenda
- [ ] The interruption queue-jump test is written verbatim
- [ ] Over-capacity items have a renegotiation owner and date, not hope
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Everything feels urgent, the to-do list has three homes plus a head, and the week keeps getting decided by whoever pings loudest. Use this to install a standing prioritization operation: one capture point, a weekly triage with honest capacity math, and a daily pick that takes five minutes because the weekly session did the thinking. This is a system to run, not a philosophy to admire - it survives on the weeks the user is too busy for it, which is exactly when it earns its keep.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Every place commitments currently live: apps, notebooks, starred emails, chat promises, the mental list
- A real week's calendar - meetings, commute, family obligations, the recurring immovables
- The current candidate list of tasks and projects, unfiltered, guilt items included
- Deadlines that are actually externally enforced vs. self-imposed
- Who is affected when something gets dropped or deferred (boss, partner, clients) and how they'd hear about it

## Workflow

1. Establish the single capture point first: one inbox (one app list or one notebook page) where every new commitment lands the moment it appears - the hallway promise, the school email, the shower idea. Multiple lists are how commitments become invisible; the rule is capture everywhere, store in exactly one place.
2. Run the capacity math before choosing anything: take the nominal work hours, subtract meetings, commute, meals, admin, and a ~20% overhead for the day's inevitable friction - the remainder is the real discretionary capacity, usually a shock (a "40-hour week" commonly yields 10-15 usable hours). Priorities chosen without this number are wishes.
3. Lay the hard landscape into the calendar before any task planning: appointments, deadlines with external teeth, obligations to other people. Only the space that remains is available for chosen work - planning tasks before fixed commitments is how Tuesday gets triple-booked.
4. Triage the full candidate list against the real capacity: pick at most three must-happen items for the week (things with consequences if missed), a short should list sized to the remaining hours, and move everything else to a later list without guilt - it's parked, not deleted. If the musts alone exceed capacity, the plan's first action is renegotiation, not optimism.
5. Install the weekly session as the keystone: same day and time, 30-45 minutes, standing agenda - empty the capture inbox to zero, review what carried over (three-time carryovers get renegotiated, shrunk, or dropped, never re-carried), redo the capacity math for the coming week, pick the new musts. Everything else in the system can wobble; this session cannot.
6. Define the daily pick: each morning (or evening before), five minutes - choose today's 1-3 tasks from the weekly musts, matched to the day's actual open slots. No re-litigating the week daily; the weekly session decided, the daily pick just schedules.
7. Set the interruption rule for mid-week arrivals: new requests go to the capture inbox by default; the only queue-jump test is "does this have worse consequences than my current musts if it waits a week?" If a genuine emergency displaces a must, the displaced item's stakeholder gets told the same day - silent slippage is how trust erodes.

## Output format

**The system on one page** - capture point, weekly session slot, daily pick time

**Capacity math** - nominal hours → subtractions itemized → real weekly capacity

**This week's triage** - musts (max 3, with consequence stated) / shoulds (sized to hours) / later list / dropped-renegotiated

**Weekly session agenda** - the standing 5-line agenda with day and time

**Interruption rule** - the queue-jump test, verbatim, plus the same-day notification rule

**Renegotiation notes** - anything over capacity: who gets told what, by when

## Do

- Keep exactly one capture point and let everything land there before judgment
- Do the subtraction honestly - capacity math with meetings left in produces fantasy weeks
- Cap musts at three; a week with seven priorities has none
- Treat the weekly session as the load-bearing habit and protect it like a client meeting

## Don't

- Don't reorganize the system when a week goes badly - tinkering with tools is procrastination wearing a productivity costume; run the same system again
- Don't fill 100% of computed capacity; plans with no slack shatter on the first surprise
- Don't let three-time carryovers ride a fourth week - renegotiate, shrink, or drop them out loud
- Don't track more than the system needs to run; metrics about the system are not progress on the work
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send renegotiation messages or decline commitments on the user's behalf without explicit approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] There is exactly one capture point, named
- [ ] Capacity is a computed number with the subtractions shown
- [ ] Musts number three or fewer and each states its miss-consequence
- [ ] The weekly session has a day, a time, and a standing agenda
- [ ] The interruption queue-jump test is written verbatim
- [ ] Over-capacity items have a renegotiation owner and date, not hope
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-risk-review
description: >
  Review operational risk for priority filters: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "priority filters risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool

# /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-risk-review

Review operational risk for priority filters: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "priority filters risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The user has a priority list - or a whole system - and wants to know where it will fail before it does. Use this to audit the current setup for the classic failure modes: commitments that were never captured anywhere, capacity math that ignores meetings, deadline collisions nobody has noticed, the one week in which three "priorities" all depend on the same person, and the quiet slide into burnout that overcommitted lists produce. The review's job is to find the two items that will actually blow up, not to grade the aesthetics of the list.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current priority list or plan, exactly as it exists (or the admission that it lives in memory)
- All the places commitments are recorded - and, crucially, made verbally or in chat but recorded nowhere
- The calendar for the period under review, including personal and family fixed points
- Dependencies: which items wait on other people, approvals, deliveries, or appointments
- Recent history: what slipped in the last month, and who was affected when it did

## Workflow

1. Hunt the invisible commitments first: things promised in hallway conversations, chat threads, or replies-to-self that appear on no list. These are the highest-likelihood failures because no system is even watching them - reconstruct them by walking the last two weeks of messages and meetings. Every one found goes on the list before any other analysis.
2. Audit the capacity assumption: compare the list's implied hours against the calendar's actual free hours after meetings, commute, family fixed points, and a friction allowance. An implied load of 30 hours against 12 real hours isn't a scheduling risk, it's a certainty of slippage - the review says which items are already dead and just don't know it.
3. Scan for collisions and clusters: deadlines landing in the same 48 hours, two big items both requiring the same evening, the week containing both the school event and the client deliverable. Collisions found now cost a renegotiation email; found later they cost a missed deadline.
4. Trace dependencies to single points of failure: items waiting on one person's reply, one approval, one delivery. For each, note the current wait time and the date at which waiting silently becomes the reason the item fails - then attach a nudge date. "Waiting on X" without a follow-up date is abandonment with better branding.
5. Check the human sustainability: count consecutive weeks at or over capacity, evenings and weekends already committed, and whether recovery time appears anywhere. An over-full list executes fine for two weeks and then fails all at once; flag the pattern, not just the items. Also flag its opposite twin - hours going into reorganizing the tracking system itself rather than the tracked work.
6. Build the risk table and rank by expected damage, not by discomfort: each risk with likelihood, impact, the earliest warning sign, a mitigation (renegotiate, drop, split, nudge, calendar-block), and an owner. End with the top two risks named bluntly and the single next action for each.

## Output format

**Top two risks** - named plainly, each with its next action and date

**Risk table**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Early warning sign | Mitigation | Owner |

**Invisible commitments found** - each one, where it was hiding, where it now lives

**Capacity verdict** - implied hours vs. real hours, and which items are already infeasible

**Collision map** - the specific dates where obligations stack

**Dependency watchlist** - item / who it waits on / nudge date

**Sustainability flag** - consecutive over-capacity weeks and the recommended relief valve

## Do

- Start with the commitments recorded nowhere - they fail first and loudest
- State the capacity verdict as arithmetic, not as encouragement
- Give every "waiting on someone" item a dated nudge
- Rank risks by expected damage; the uncomfortable renegotiation usually outranks the tidy calendar fix

## Don't

- Don't audit only what's on the list - the list's omissions are the main findings
- Don't accept "I'll find the time" as a mitigation; time is found by removing something, name what
- Don't treat a color-coded tracker as evidence of low risk - tracking polish and actual slack are unrelated
- Don't recommend a new app as a mitigation; system-switching mid-overload adds a migration to an overloaded week
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't renegotiate or cancel commitments on the user's behalf - every outbound renegotiation gets the user's explicit approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Invisible commitments were actively hunted, not just asked about
- [ ] The capacity verdict shows the arithmetic
- [ ] Every dependency has a nudge date
- [ ] Collisions are mapped to specific dates
- [ ] The top two risks each have one concrete next action
- [ ] Assumptions and unknowns are marked as such
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-stakeholder-brief
description: >
  Prepare stakeholder communication for priority filters: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "priority filters stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-stakeholder-brief.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for priority filters: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "priority filters stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The user has re-prioritized - honestly, with capacity math - and now other people need to hear what that means for them: the boss whose project moves to next month, the client whose deliverable shifts a week, the partner who's covering Thursday evenings for a while, the committee that's losing a volunteer. Use this to write those notifications before anything slips silently. The difference between a renegotiation and a broken promise is almost entirely timing: the same delay announced two weeks early reads as professionalism, announced two days late reads as failure.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The reprioritization itself: what's staying, what's moving, what's being dropped
- Each affected person, what they were expecting, and by when
- What the user can genuinely still offer each of them: a new date, a reduced scope, a handoff, or a clean no
- Power and stakes per relationship - the note to a manager and the note to a book club read differently
- Anything already promised in writing, which constrains how the change can be framed

## Workflow

1. Sort stakeholders by what they lose, not by who they are: someone losing a deadline they built plans on outranks someone losing a nice-to-have, whatever the org chart says. Notify the biggest-loss people first and individually; batch announcements are for low-stakes groups only.
2. For each person, lead with the concrete change in their terms - "the report you're expecting Friday will now come Wednesday the 16th" - before any explanation. People forgive a change they understand in the first sentence; they distrust one buried under three paragraphs of context about workload.
3. Give one honest reason, sized to the relationship, and stop: over-explaining reads as guilt and invites negotiation of the reason instead of the plan. "I've taken on the audit and had to re-sequence" is complete; the inner workings of the capacity spreadsheet are not their problem.
4. Pair every takeaway with a real offer: the new date (one the math actually supports, with slack - a renegotiated deadline missed twice is worse than the original slip), a reduced-scope version delivered on time, or a named handoff. If the honest offer is nothing, say a clean no now rather than a slow-motion one over six weeks.
5. Pre-decide the pushback line: what the user will do if the stakeholder says "that doesn't work for me" - where there's flex, where there isn't, and what trades are acceptable ("I can do yours by Friday if the Nelson review moves"). Never re-promise on the spot under social pressure; "let me confirm tomorrow" is always available.
6. Sequence the sends so nobody important hears it secondhand - the manager before the teammate who'd mention it, the co-lead before the group thread - and calendar a follow-through check for each new commitment made, because a renegotiated date that then slips quietly burns the credibility the brief just spent.

## Output format

**Change summary** - what moved, what dropped, what stayed, in four lines

**Stakeholder table** - person / what they lose / notify by (date) / channel / individually or batched

**Per-person messages** - send-ready text for each high-stakes stakeholder: the change in sentence one, one reason, the offer, the ask for confirmation

**Pushback plan** - per stakeholder: where there's flex, where there isn't, acceptable trades

**Send order** - who hears first and why

**Follow-through calendar** - each new date promised, with the user's own check-in reminder before it

## Do

- Notify before the slip, in the stakeholder's own terms, change first
- Make every new date one the capacity math survives - pad it
- Offer something real with every takeaway, even when the something is a clean no
- Send individually to anyone whose plans depended on you

## Don't

- Don't explain the whole prioritization system; one sized reason, then the plan
- Don't re-promise on the spot under pushback - confirm the next day, after checking capacity
- Don't let anyone senior or close hear the change from a third party
- Don't dress a drop as a delay; if it's not coming back, say so and close it kindly
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send any of these messages on the user's behalf without explicit approval - the user reviews and sends each one

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every affected person appears in the table with a notify-by date
- [ ] Each message states the concrete change in the first sentence
- [ ] Every new date offered has slack behind it, not hope
- [ ] The pushback plan exists before any message goes out
- [ ] The send order protects high-stakes relationships from secondhand news
- [ ] Follow-through reminders are calendared for every new promise
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-priority-filter-stakeholder-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

# /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for family schedules: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "family schedules decision-memo", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The family calendar has a collision that won't resolve itself: the second kid wants an activity that lands on the already-full Thursday, both parents have a Tuesday commitment and someone must do pickup, or the question is whether to add travel soccer knowing it eats every other weekend. These are load-bearing decisions dressed as scheduling questions. Use this to decide with the whole family's capacity on the table - including the driver's - instead of by whoever asks most persistently.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The specific conflict or proposed addition, and its full true footprint: sessions, travel time, gear runs, tournaments, fundraising
- The current weekly grid - every family member's fixed commitments, including the adults' work realities
- Who drives, who covers, and how loaded each adult already is (the honest version)
- What the affected child (or adult) actually wants versus what's assumed they want
- What could be traded away: is anything on the current schedule zombie - attended out of momentum, enjoyed by no one?

## Workflow

1. Price the proposal fully. An activity is never just its sessions: add travel each way, gear and fee costs, tournament weekends, and whose evening it consumes. Write the true weekly cost in hours, per person.
2. Check adult capacity first, not last. Kids' schedules break at the driver, not the kid. If the addition needs a fourth Thursday driver-hour from a parent who has zero, that constraint decides - unless something else moves.
3. Hunt for the zombie commitment. Before rejecting the new thing, test the old ones: anything continued purely by momentum is trade material. Frame the decision as a swap where possible - swaps are easier for everyone to accept than flat denials.
4. Lay out the 2-3 real options: yes as proposed, yes with a modification (the closer club, the every-other-week version, carpool splitting), or not this season with a named revisit point. "Not never - not now" is a legitimate option and should be written that way.
5. Weigh family-level costs, not just the requester's benefit: the sibling who loses their ride, the shared dinner that disappears twice a week, the parent whose only free evening vanishes. Name these plainly - they're usually the real objection nobody voices.
6. Recommend one option and state its price honestly, plus the condition under which it gets revisited (end of season, if grades slip, if the carpool falls through).

## Output format

**Decision** - one sentence: the addition/conflict and the recommended call

**True footprint** - hours per week, per person, including travel and the hidden extras

**Capacity check** - each adult's load before and after; the binding constraint named

**Options**

| Option | Weekly cost (whose hours) | What it displaces | Who bears the downside |

**Recommendation** - the call, its honest price, and any swap that funds it

**Revisit point** - when and on what evidence this gets reconsidered

## Do

- Cost activities at their full footprint - travel, gear, tournaments - not their advertised hour
- Treat driver and coordinator hours as the scarcest resource in the family, because they are
- Offer a modified yes or a dated not-now before a flat no; both preserve the relationship with the asker
- Check what the child actually wants - sometimes the activity is the parent's dream, and that changes the math

## Don't

- Don't decide by squeaky wheel - the quiet kid's standing commitments count as much as the loud request
- Don't solve the conflict by silently overdrawing one parent's capacity; that budget always comes due
- Don't stack "just one more thing" onto the only unscheduled evening - a family with zero blank space has no shock absorber
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The footprint includes travel time and hidden extras, per person
- [ ] Adult capacity before/after is explicit and the binding constraint is named
- [ ] At least one option is a swap or a modified yes, not just yes/no
- [ ] Family-level costs (siblings, shared meals, the free evening) are stated
- [ ] The recommendation has a revisit point with a date or trigger
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-decision-memo or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for family schedules: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "family schedules operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for family schedules: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "family schedules operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Family logistics live in four places - one parent's head, a school app, a group chat, and a paper flyer on the fridge - and the failure mode is always the same: a pickup nobody owned, a permission slip discovered Sunday night, two parents both assuming the other had Thursday. Use this to build the family's weekly operating rhythm: one shared calendar as the single source of truth, a named owner on every moving part, and a 15-minute weekly sync that catches collisions before they happen.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Everyone in the system: adults, kids, and regular helpers (grandparent, sitter, carpool families)
- All recurring commitments per person, with locations and real door-to-door times
- Where schedule information currently arrives (school apps, team chats, paper) and who sees each
- The current tooling and its failure: which calendar, who actually checks it, what fell through last month
- Meal, homework, and bedtime anchors that the schedule must protect

## Workflow

1. Declare one source of truth. Pick a single shared calendar every adult can see and edit; a color per person. The rule that makes it work: if it's not on the calendar, it isn't happening - and nobody is obligated to remember it.
2. Load the recurring skeleton first - school, work fixtures, activities, the standing carpool - with true door-to-door times, not optimistic ones. Recurring events get recurring entries; retyping Tuesday swim every week is how Tuesday swim gets lost.
3. Put an owner on every leg, not every event. "Swim 5pm" is information; "Mom drives there, carpool brings home" is a plan. Every drop-off, pickup, and waiting period has exactly one name on it - including the helper legs, confirmed with the helper.
4. Assign inbox duty: every information source (each school app, each team chat, the flyer folder) has one named adult who transfers dates onto the calendar within a day of arrival. Unowned inboxes are where permission slips go to die.
5. Protect the anchors. Block family dinner nights, homework windows, and one genuinely empty weekend slot on the calendar before the week fills - they're commitments, not leftovers, and they only survive if they're visible.
6. Install the weekly sync: 15 minutes, same time each week (Sunday evening works), both adults, calendar open. Agenda never changes - walk the week day by day, name the owner of every leg, flag the collisions, decide the swaps, check the money-and-forms list (fees due, slips, gear).
7. Add the daily glance: a one-minute look at tomorrow the night before, plus a visible version for kids old enough to read it - kids who can see the plan stop asking, and start packing their own bags.

## Output format

**Family schedule system - [family name]**

**Source of truth** - the calendar, who's on it, color per person, and the "not on it = not happening" rule

**Weekly recurring grid**

| Day | Time | Who | What | Drives there | Brings home |

**Inbox duty** - each information source → the adult who owns transferring it

**Protected anchors** - the blocks that fill in before anything else

**Weekly sync** - day, time, and the standing four-line agenda

**Escalation habit** - how a mid-week change gets in: whoever learns of it puts it on the calendar and tags the affected adult, immediately

## Do

- Assign owners to legs (each drive, each pickup), not just to events
- Enter true door-to-door times - the 6:00 practice that requires a 5:20 departure is a 5:20 commitment
- Confirm helper legs with the helper before writing their name on them
- Keep the weekly sync to 15 minutes with a fixed agenda; the moment it becomes a meeting, it dies

## Don't

- Don't run a two-calendar system "temporarily" - the day one adult trusts a different source, the single source of truth is fiction
- Don't let one parent silently remain the human backup database; if the system only works because of their memory, there is no system
- Don't schedule the family to 100% - the blank evening is the shock absorber for the sick day and the forgotten project
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Exactly one source of truth, and every adult can both see and edit it
- [ ] Every leg of every recurring commitment has one named owner
- [ ] Every information inbox (apps, chats, paper) has a named transfer owner
- [ ] Anchors and at least one empty slot are blocked before the week fills
- [ ] The weekly sync exists with a day, time, and fixed agenda
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-risk-review
description: >
  Review operational risk for family schedules: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "family schedules risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand

  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home

---
# /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-risk-review

Review operational risk for family schedules: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "family schedules risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The family schedule works - until one thing wobbles. One sick kid, one delayed flight, one carpool parent's dead battery, and the whole week dominos, usually onto the same parent. Use this to stress-test the weekly grid before it fails: find the single points of failure (usually one driver and one memory), the days with zero slack, and build the backup web - because families don't get to reschedule the school run.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current weekly grid with owners per leg
- Which legs have exactly one possible adult, and which days run back-to-back with no slack
- The dependency map on outsiders: carpools, sitters, grandparents - and how reliable each has actually been
- What broke in the last three months, and who absorbed it each time
- Standing constraints: a job that can't flex, a co-parenting agreement, a kid who can't be late for meds or therapy

## Workflow

1. Run the sick-day test first: for each weekday, ask "a kid wakes up at 7am with a fever - what happens?" If the answer is "one specific parent cancels their day, again," write that down as the system's load-bearing wall.
2. Map single points of failure: every leg with exactly one capable adult, every piece of schedule knowledge living in exactly one head, every carpool with no fallback. Each is one flat tire from a stranded kid.
3. Find the zero-slack days - back-to-back legs where a 15-minute delay cascades. Mark the domino chains: which delay knocks over which subsequent commitment.
4. Check the absorption ledger: tally who took the hit for each of the last few disruptions. If it's the same parent every time, the risk isn't just logistical - the buffer person is burning out, and that's the biggest risk on the page.
5. Build the backup web for the top exposures: a second authorized pickup person per kid (school paperwork done in advance), a reciprocal emergency pact with one other family, a sitter list that's actually current, and written handoff notes for the legs only one person knows how to run.
6. Pre-agree the triage rule for collision days: which commitments drop first (social), which flex (activities), which never move (medical, custody exchanges, the thing the kid has a solo in). Deciding this calm prevents deciding it at 7:05am.
7. Fix what's cheap to fix now: file the second-pickup authorization, share the calendar with grandma, write the Thursday-run instructions - each closes a failure mode for under 20 minutes of effort.

## Output format

**Grid under review** - one line on the current system

**Risk table**

| Risk | Day/leg exposed | Likelihood | Who absorbs it today | Backup (named person, arranged in advance) |

**Single points of failure** - legs, knowledge, and outsider dependencies with no fallback

**Domino chains** - the zero-slack sequences and where each cascade stops

**Absorption ledger** - who has been eating the disruptions; rebalance move if it's lopsided

**Triage rule** - drops first / flexes / never moves

**Fix-now list** - the under-20-minute arrangements to make this week

## Do

- Treat "one parent always absorbs it" as a top risk, not a working system
- Arrange backups before they're needed - school pickup authorizations and key handoffs can't be done at 7am
- Keep the backup web reciprocal; a one-way emergency pact quietly expires
- Test knowledge transfer: could the other adult run Thursday from what's written down?

## Don't

- Don't call heroics a plan - a system that survives only because someone cancels their work is already failing, just quietly
- Don't build backups for rare disasters while ignoring the weekly near-miss (the chronically late carpool, the practice that always overruns)
- Don't leave the triage hierarchy implicit; 7am is the worst possible time to negotiate whose commitment matters more
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every weekday passes the sick-day test with a named, pre-arranged answer
- [ ] Every single point of failure has a backup person or a written handoff
- [ ] The absorption imbalance is surfaced, not smoothed over
- [ ] The triage rule ranks commitments explicitly, including "never moves"
- [ ] The fix-now list contains only actions doable this week
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

5. Build the backup web for the top exposures: a second authorized pickup person per kid (school paperwork done in advance), a reciprocal emergency pact with one other family, a sitter list that's actually current, and written handoff notes for the legs only one person knows how to run.
6. Pre-agree the triage rule for collision days: which commitments drop first (social), which flex (activities), which never move (medical, custody exchanges, the thing the kid has a solo in). Deciding this calm prevents deciding it at 7:05am.
7. Fix what's cheap to fix now: file the second-pickup authorization, share the calendar with grandma, write the Thursday-run instructions - each closes a failure mode for under 20 minutes of effort.

## Output format

**Grid under review** - one line on the current system

**Risk table**

| Risk | Day/leg exposed | Likelihood | Who absorbs it today | Backup (named person, arranged in advance) |

**Single points of failure** - legs, knowledge, and outsider dependencies with no fallback

**Domino chains** - the zero-slack sequences and where each cascade stops

**Absorption ledger** - who has been eating the disruptions; rebalance move if it's lopsided

**Triage rule** - drops first / flexes / never moves

**Fix-now list** - the under-20-minute arrangements to make this week

## Do

- Treat "one parent always absorbs it" as a top risk, not a working system
- Arrange backups before they're needed - school pickup authorizations and key handoffs can't be done at 7am
- Keep the backup web reciprocal; a one-way emergency pact quietly expires
- Test knowledge transfer: could the other adult run Thursday from what's written down?

## Don't

- Don't call heroics a plan - a system that survives only because someone cancels their work is already failing, just quietly
- Don't build backups for rare disasters while ignoring the weekly near-miss (the chronically late carpool, the practice that always overruns)
- Don't leave the triage hierarchy implicit; 7am is the worst possible time to negotiate whose commitment matters more
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every weekday passes the sick-day test with a named, pre-arranged answer
- [ ] Every single point of failure has a backup person or a written handoff
- [ ] The absorption imbalance is surfaced, not smoothed over
- [ ] The triage rule ranks commitments explicitly, including "never moves"
- [ ] The fix-now list contains only actions doable this week
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-stakeholder-brief
description: >
  Prepare stakeholder communication for family schedules: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "family schedules stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-stakeholder-brief.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for family schedules: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "family schedules stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The week's plan exists in the parents' heads and the shared calendar - but the people executing it include a grandmother who doesn't use apps, a carpool parent who needs Thursday confirmed, a sitter who needs addresses and allergy notes, and two kids who will ask "who's picking me up?" at 7:45am regardless. Use this to publish the schedule in the formats its actual audience can use: the fridge-door week, the helper one-pagers, and the confirmation texts that keep the carpool alive.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The finalized week: every leg with times, places, and owners
- The audience list and what each can actually use: apps, texts, paper, big-print
- What each helper needs beyond times: addresses, gate codes, gear, meds/allergies, dinner instructions, backup contact
- Which legs are unconfirmed and become real only when someone replies
- Anything unusual this week versus routine - deviations are the whole message for regulars

## Workflow

1. Split the audience into executors (they perform legs: co-parent, grandma, sitter, carpool) and consumers (they follow it: the kids). Executors get personal briefs; consumers get the visible week.
2. Confirm before publishing: every leg owned by a non-household helper gets a yes/no confirmation text *first* - "Still good for Thursday 3:15 pickup at the gym entrance?" A schedule built on unconfirmed favors is a rumor.
3. Write each helper's one-pager with only their legs, in their format: exact times and places, the kid-specific notes (allergy, medication timing, homework rule, gate code), what to do if the kid isn't out by 3:30, and the backup adult's number. Assume they know nothing that isn't written.
4. Make the fridge version: the week as a single glanceable grid, kid-readable - day, activity, who takes, who brings home, what to pack. Symbols or colors for pre-readers. This artifact answers "who's getting me?" so the parents don't have to, five times.
5. Highlight deviations loudly for people who know the routine: "NOTE: Wednesday is early dismissal - pickup 1:30 not 3:15." Regulars skim; only the difference from normal reaches them.
6. Close the loop on changes mid-week: any change gets pushed to exactly the affected people ("Thursday swim cancelled - no pickup needed, enjoy the hour back"), not broadcast to everyone, and the fridge version gets corrected the same day.

## Output format

**Week of [date] - schedule communications**

**Confirmation texts** (send first) - one per unconfirmed leg, yes/no answerable

**Helper one-pagers** - per person: their legs only · times and exact places · kid notes (allergies, meds, rules) · if-things-go-wrong contact and instruction

**Fridge week** - glanceable grid: day / activity / who takes / who brings home / pack list

**Deviation callouts** - the differences from routine, stated as differences

**Change-push protocol** - who gets told what when something moves mid-week

## Do

- Confirm helper legs before publishing them as facts
- Put the failure instruction on every helper sheet - "if she's not out by 3:30, call me, don't leave" prevents the worst afternoon
- Write for the least-technical executor; grandma's paper sheet outranks the app in load-bearing importance
- Keep kid-facing versions answer-shaped: who, when, what to pack

## Don't

- Don't broadcast the whole family calendar to every helper - each person gets their legs and the notes they need, nothing else
- Don't share kids' medical or custody details beyond the person who needs them for their specific leg
- Don't let a mid-week change live only in the group chat; correct the fridge version or accept that half the family is on the old plan
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send anything on the user's behalf without explicit approval - messages to helpers and co-parents are a sensitive action

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every non-household leg has a confirmation text that precedes publication
- [ ] Each helper sheet is self-sufficient: places, notes, failure instruction, backup number
- [ ] The fridge version answers "who's getting me and what do I pack" per day
- [ ] Deviations from routine are called out as deviations
- [ ] Sensitive kid details go only to the person who needs them; nothing sends without user approval
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-family-schedule-stakeholder-brief or describe the task from the skill description.
