# /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for errand batching: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "errand batching operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The batch is decided - now it needs to survive contact with reality. A run of five stops fails on small things: arriving at the bank at 12:40 when it closes at 1 on Saturdays, reaching the returns desk without the receipt, hitting the pharmacy before the refill is ready. Use this to turn an errand list into a sequenced run with opening hours checked, prerequisites staged by the door, and a route that starts at the tightest constraint.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The confirmed errand list for the run, with locations
- Opening hours and quirks per stop (lunch closures, weekend counters, appointment-only services)
- Prerequisites per errand: receipts, ID, reference numbers, the actual item to return, containers, cards
- Transport mode and any cold-chain or bulk constraints (frozen groceries last, heavy items near the car)
- Who else is available - a second adult or a teen driver splits a run in half

## Workflow

1. Verify hours before routing. Check each stop's actual hours for the chosen day - Saturday hours lie in memory more than any other fact in errand planning. Anything appointment-only gets booked now.
2. Find the anchor: the stop with the narrowest window or hardest constraint (the counter that shuts at noon, the booked slot). The run is built around reaching the anchor comfortably, not around geographic elegance.
3. Sequence the rest by geography *and* physics: minimize backtracking, but frozen and refrigerated items go last, heavy or bulky pickups go nearest the end, and the stop most likely to queue gets morning placement.
4. Stage the prerequisites tonight, not on the way out: one bag by the door with every receipt, return item, ID, and reference number - checked against the list per stop. A forgotten receipt converts a 5-minute stop into a second trip.
5. Time-box each stop with honest numbers (the post office is not 5 minutes) and add 20% slack overall. If the total exceeds the available window, cut the least-constrained stop now rather than abandoning the run mid-way.
6. Split ownership where a second person exists: divide by geography or by queue-heaviness, and agree the handoff point and time.
7. Define the bail rule in advance: which stops are must-complete today, and which drop without guilt if the run overruns - so a slow queue doesn't sink the whole plan or ruin the day.

## Output format

**Run: [day, date] - window [start-end]**

**Route**

| # | Stop | Address/area | Hours checked | Window | Bring | Est. time | Must-do or droppable |

**Anchor** - the constraint the route is built around

**Staging list** - everything in the bag by the door the night before

**Split** - who takes which stops, handoff point and time (if two people)

**Bail rule** - what drops first if the run overruns, and what must not

**Not this run** - errands deliberately left for another route

## Do

- Build the route around the tightest constraint, not the shortest total distance
- Confirm hours from the source (site or a call), especially for government counters and independent shops
- Stage everything the night before - the run is won or lost at the front door
- Add slack; a run planned at 100% density fails on the first queue

## Don't

- Don't put frozen goods or the pharmacy pickup first because they're closest - physics and window order both outrank proximity
- Don't plan more stops than the window honestly holds and call the overflow "if there's time" without a rule for it
- Don't turn a 90-minute run into a full-day expedition by adding "while I'm out" stops mid-run - the bail rule cuts both ways
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every stop has verified hours for the specific day of the run
- [ ] The anchor constraint is named and the sequence protects it
- [ ] Each errand lists its physical prerequisites and they appear on the staging list
- [ ] Total estimated time plus 20% slack fits the available window
- [ ] The bail rule names droppable stops in order
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

# /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-risk-review

Review operational risk for errand batching: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "errand batching risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A batched run is planned - and batching concentrates failure: one dead car battery, one closed counter, or one missing document can now take five errands down together instead of one. Use this before a run that includes anything with a hard deadline or real money attached (a closing return window, a document that must be filed, a deposit in person) to find the single points of failure and cheap insurance for each.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The planned run: stops, sequence, window, and who's driving
- Which errands are deadline-critical or money-critical if they slip past this run
- Dependencies between stops (cash from the bank needed at stop three; the return receipt printed at home)
- How previous runs failed - the queue that ate an hour, the counter that wanted a document nobody mentioned
- Backup resources: second driver, alternate day this week, delivery fallback

## Workflow

1. Separate the stakes: mark each stop as critical (real cost if missed today) or convenience. The review protects the critical ones; the convenience stops are the crumple zone.
2. Find the chain dependencies - any stop whose success depends on an earlier stop or a thing from home. Chains fail at the weakest link; every chain gets either a decoupling (do it separately) or a double-check at staging.
3. Interrogate the assumptions per critical stop: is the item actually in stock or ready for pickup? does the counter want a document, a code, the original card used for purchase? A two-minute call or app check today beats discovering it at the desk.
4. Rate the timing risk: which stop is most likely to blow the schedule (the queue-prone one, the "quick" government office)? If it sits early in the route, everything after it inherits the risk - consider moving it last or to its own trip.
5. Attach cheap insurance to each critical stop: confirmation call, screenshot of the barcode/reference, second acceptable location, or an alternate day already identified in this week's calendar.
6. Write the contingency line per critical errand: "If [stop] fails today, then [specific fallback] by [date]" - so a failed stop costs a plan B, not a spiral.

## Output format

**Run under review** - day, stops, window

**Critical vs convenience** - the split, one line each

**Risk table**

| Risk | Stop(s) affected | Likelihood | Cost if it hits | Cheap insurance (done before the run) |

**Chain dependencies** - what depends on what, and the decoupling or staging check for each

**Contingency lines** - per critical errand: if it fails today → fallback → by when

**Verdict** - run as planned / resequence / split the critical stop into its own trip

## Do

- Spend the review effort on the two or three stops where failure has a real price
- Prefer insurance that costs minutes today (a call, a screenshot, a stock check) over cleverness during the run
- Pull a deadline-critical errand out of a long batch when the batch itself is the risk
- Use the history of past runs - the stop that burned an hour last time will again

## Don't

- Don't risk-manage the dry cleaning like a court filing - flat effort across unequal stakes is its own failure
- Don't let one fragile stop early in the route hold the rest hostage; resequence or split instead
- Don't rely on "they're always open" or "it'll be ready" for anything critical - verify or plan as if unverified
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Critical and convenience stops are explicitly separated
- [ ] Every chain dependency has a decoupling or a staging check
- [ ] Each critical stop has insurance executable before the run starts
- [ ] Every contingency line names a fallback and a date, not "try again sometime"
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

# /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for errand batching: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "errand batching stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An errand run touches other people: the partner who needs to know the car is gone from 9 to noon, the neighbor being asked to accept a delivery, the household members who must hand over their returns and prescriptions *before* the run or forever hold their peace. Use this to write the short coordination messages that stop the run from being interrupted, duplicated ("I was already at that store yesterday"), or immediately followed by "oh, while you were out, could you have-".

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The planned run: when, roughly where, and how long
- Who is affected: shares the car or childcare, expects the user reachable, or has items to add
- What the user needs from each person, with a cutoff time (items and lists in by tonight)
- Any pickups or favors being requested of others (neighbor, friend passing the store)
- Whether anyone else was already planning an overlapping trip this week

## Workflow

1. List who's touched by the run in three buckets: resources shared (car, childcare cover), contributions owed (their returns, their lists, their prescriptions), and favors asked (the neighbor, the friend already going).
2. Send the contribution call first, with a hard cutoff: "Anything for Saturday's run gets to the hall table by Friday 8pm. After that it waits for next time." The cutoff is what makes batching work - enforce it kindly and literally.
3. Check for overlap before finalizing: one message to the household asking who's already passing which shops this week routinely deletes two stops from the run.
4. Write the coverage note for whoever's holding the fort: window, the one or two stops where the user is unreachable (queues, driving), and when they're back - so a non-urgent "can you grab milk" text doesn't reroute the run mid-flight.
5. Make each favor-ask self-contained and refusable: exactly what, exactly where, by when, with the reference or barcode attached, and an easy out ("no problem at all if not").
6. Close the loop after the run with a one-liner: what got done, what didn't and its plan B, and whose items came back - so nobody re-asks about a thing that's already handled.

## Output format

**Run coordination - [day, window]**

**Contribution call** (household, ready to send) - the ask, the collection point, the cutoff, and the "after cutoff it waits" line

**Coverage note** - who holds the fort, the window, the reachability gap, return time

**Favor asks** (one per person, ready to send) - the specific item and place, deadline, attachment noted, easy-out line

**Overlap check** - the one message that asks who's already going where this week

**After-run close-out** - template: done / not done + plan B / items returned to whom

## Do

- Give every ask a cutoff time and hold it - a batch with a soft deadline reopens all day
- Make favor requests specific enough to complete without a follow-up question
- Tell the household when the user is unreachable so mid-run additions don't happen by text
- Send the close-out; it's ten seconds and it prevents next week's duplicate errand

## Don't

- Don't accept "while you're out" additions after the cutoff - say the line, add it to next run's list, move on
- Don't ask favors vaguely ("if you happen to be near a pharmacy...") - vague asks create obligation without success
- Don't let the coordination message balloon into a household logistics essay; each note is under 30 seconds to read
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send anything on the user's behalf without explicit approval - messages to real people are a sensitive action

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The contribution call names a place, a cutoff, and the consequence of missing it
- [ ] Every favor-ask is completable without a clarifying question and includes an easy out
- [ ] The coverage note states the unreachable window plainly
- [ ] A close-out template exists so completed errands don't get re-asked
- [ ] Nothing is sent without the user's approval; any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-errand-batching-stakeholder-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

# /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-decision-memo

Write a decision memo for life admin inboxes: a clear recommendation with tradeoffs. Produces a copy-ready decision memo. Triggers: "life admin inboxes decision-memo", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-decision-memo.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The life-admin backlog has become a standing decision the user keeps not making: the drawer of unopened mail, the "deal with later" email folder, the insurance renewal auto-renewing at whatever price for the third year. The real question isn't "should I sort this" - it's *how this household's admin should run from now on*: paper vs digital filing, autopay vs manual review, one owner vs shared duty, keep vs shred. Use this to make those structural calls once, so the pile stops regenerating.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current state, honestly: where admin accumulates (drawer, counter, email folders), and roughly how old the oldest unopened item is
- What has actually gone wrong so far: late fees paid, a lapsed policy, a missed school form, a fine that escalated
- Who touches admin today, and whether that's by agreement or by default (the "whoever cracks first" system)
- Volume and shape: mostly bills? mostly kid paperwork? a small business's receipts mixed in?
- Comfort with digital tools, autopay, and paperless billing - the user's real comfort, not the aspirational one

## Workflow

1. Diagnose before prescribing. A pile has one of three root causes and each takes a different fix: no *place* (things land wherever), no *time* (a place exists but is never processed), or no *decider* (two adults each assume the other has it). Name which one this household has - most "messy people" turn out to have a structure problem, not a character problem.
2. Decide the ownership question first, because everything else hangs on it: one admin owner with a deputy for absences, or split domains (one takes money, the other takes school/medical). Recommend against unassigned sharing - in admin, "both of us" reliably means neither.
3. Make the autopay call category by category, not wholesale: fixed, predictable bills (rent, utilities within known range) go autopay with a monthly statement glance; variable or dispute-prone ones (medical, anything usage-billed) stay manual-review. Flag the renewal trap explicitly - insurance and subscriptions that auto-renew unreviewed are the pile's most expensive residents.
4. Make the paper-vs-digital call by retrieval, not by tidiness: the test is "could we find this in five minutes during a crisis?" Recommend the smallest filing scheme that passes - typically one physical folder set for originals that must stay paper (IDs, deeds, certificates) and one scanned archive for everything else. Ten categories maximum; a 40-folder taxonomy is a hobby, not a system.
5. Set the keep/shred defaults so each future item is a lookup, not a deliberation: identity and legal originals forever; tax-relevant records per local retention guidance (name that the user should confirm the years for their jurisdiction); statements and bills only until reconciled unless tax-relevant; manuals and expired policies out.
6. Recommend the package as one coherent system - owner, autopay map, filing scheme, keep/shred card - with the tradeoffs stated (autopay trades control for reliability; digital trades ritual for retrieval) and the one-month review date to adjust what chafes.

## Output format

**Ownership call** - who owns admin (or which domains), deputy named, and what "owner" means in a sentence

**Autopay map**

| Category | Autopay or manual | Review rhythm | Renewal-trap flag |

**Filing call** - paper set (what stays physical) + digital archive (how scanned, where), retrieval test stated

**Keep/shred defaults** - the one-card version, with jurisdiction-check note for tax years

**Tradeoffs accepted** - two or three lines, honest

**Review date** - one month out, with the question "what chafes?"

## Do

- Fix the diagnosed cause; a beautiful filing system doesn't help a no-time household
- Put a monthly statement glance behind every autopay - automation without review is how the gym membership from 2022 survives
- Size the system to the household's real digital comfort, not to the best-reviewed app
- Keep the category count small enough to memorize

## Don't

- Don't recommend sorting the backlog as the answer - the backlog is a symptom; this memo fixes the machine that produced it
- Don't put variable or dispute-prone bills on autopay to feel finished
- Don't design a system that only its architect can operate; the deputy must be able to run it cold
- Don't give definitive tax-retention or legal-document advice - state sensible defaults and direct the user to confirm rules for their jurisdiction
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The filing scheme passes the five-minute crisis-retrieval test and stays under ten categories
- [ ] Keep/shred defaults fit on one card, with the jurisdiction caveat present
- [ ] A named owner (or domain split) exists - no unassigned sharing
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

```markdown
---
name: personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-operating-plan
description: >
  Build an operating plan for life admin inboxes: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "life admin inboxes operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-operating-plan.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Finance, Legal, People & Home
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-operating-plan

Build an operating plan for life admin inboxes: a practical execution plan with owners. Produces a copy-ready operating plan. Triggers: "life admin inboxes operating-plan", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-operating-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

There's a backlog (the drawer, the counter pile, the 400-message "important" folder) *and* a daily inflow that keeps refilling it. Use this to run both operations: a triage blitz that finds the dangerous items first, and a standing weekly admin block that keeps the pile from regenerating. The plan works in severity order - money and legal before everything - because the risk in a paper pile is never the volume, it's the two items in it with deadlines.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Every accumulation point: physical piles, email folders, app notification backlogs, the glovebox
- Anything the user already suspects is urgent or scary in there - the envelope they've been avoiding
- The filing/ownership decisions if already made (owner, autopay map, folders); otherwise minimal defaults get set here
- The weekly calendar: where a recurring 30-45 minute block can actually live
- Tools on hand: shredder or shredding option, scanner or phone scan app, label situation

## Workflow

1. Gather everything into one inbox - every pile, drawer, bag, and the flagged emails printed to a list - one physical tray plus one digital folder. Do not sort while gathering; do not read while gathering. The single-capture-point rule starts here and never ends: from today, incoming admin lands in the tray, nowhere else.
2. Run the danger scan first, 20 minutes, one pass: pull anything from government, court, tax authority, insurer, lender, or collections, and anything with a visible deadline. This stack is the actual emergency; the rest of the pile is just storage. Open the scary envelope first - its contents are almost always cheaper than its interest.
3. Triage the danger stack immediately: for each item - deadline passed? (act today: call, pay, or arrange a plan; escalation is priced by the week) / deadline coming? (calendar it with a 3-day-early alarm, then file to an "active" folder) / informational? (file). Anything genuinely alarming (legal action, large debt) gets flagged for professional advice, not heroics.
4. Process the remainder in timed sessions - 45 minutes, not "until done" - touching each item once with a forced verdict: **do now** (under 2 minutes: pay it, reply, toss), **date it** (needs action: onto the calendar or the short action list - "deal with car insurance" is not an action; "call insurer for renewal quote before the 14th" is), **file it** (no action, worth keeping), **shred/recycle** (the honest majority).
5. Expect the backlog to take 2-4 sessions across a week or two - schedule them. A single heroic Sunday produces a half-sorted pile and a resolution; scheduled sessions produce an empty tray.
6. Install the weekly admin block: same day, same 30-45 minutes, standing agenda - empty the tray to zero, pay/schedule what surfaced, glance at statements for autopay surprises, check the "active" folder for items coming due, shred what's done. This block is the keystone; every skipped week is a new pile deposit.
7. Reduce the inflow while processing: unsubscribe from every catalog and marketing sender encountered, switch survivable bills to paperless-plus-autopay per the household's map, and put a "no new subscriptions without a calendar note of the renewal date" rule in place.

## Output format

**Admin operation - [household] - start date**

**Inbox** - the one tray + one folder, location named; the "everything lands here" rule

**Danger stack triage** - item / sender / deadline / action taken or scheduled

**Backlog schedule** - sessions booked (date, 45 min each), with expected finish

**Verdict rules** - the four verdicts, with the 2-minute and next-action-phrasing rules stated

**Weekly block** - day, time, standing 5-line agenda

**Inflow reductions** - unsubscribed / switched to paperless / renewal-note rule

**Active folder** - items awaiting a dated action, each with its calendar entry

## Do

- Find the dangerous two items before perfecting the filing of the harmless two hundred
- Touch each item once and force a verdict - re-piling "to decide later" is the pile reproducing
- Phrase every deferred item as a next action with a date and put it on the calendar, not in a stack
- Protect the weekly block like an appointment; it is the difference between a system and a cleanup

## Don't

- Don't sort by category before scanning for danger - a beautifully organized pile can still contain the unpaid fine
- Don't marathon it; timed sessions with a booked next session beat the all-day purge that ends in fatigue and a new drawer
- Don't keep things because keeping feels safer - apply the keep/shred defaults and let the shredder work
- Don't handle identity documents casually: shred anything with account numbers or personal data rather than recycling it readable
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions - payments, calls to creditors, and account changes get the user's explicit go-ahead

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The danger scan happens before any categorizing
- [ ] Every dated obligation found is on the calendar with an early alarm, not just noted
- [ ] Backlog sessions are scheduled with dates, not implied
- [ ] The weekly block exists with day, time, and standing agenda
- [ ] Deferred items are phrased as next actions ("call X about Y by Z")
- [ ] Anything legal/debt-serious is flagged for professional advice
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-operating-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

description: >
  Review operational risk for life admin inboxes: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "life admin inboxes risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-risk-review.
metadata:
  category: personal
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool

# /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-risk-review

Review operational risk for life admin inboxes: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "life admin inboxes risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Life admin fails silently and bills you later: the passport discovered expired three weeks before the trip, the insurance that lapsed with the changed card number, the tax letter that aged from reminder to penalty in a drawer. Use this to audit a household's admin exposure - where the deadline-bearing items live, which renewals can lapse unnoticed, what breaks if the one admin-competent adult is unavailable - and to close the cheap gaps before one of them invoices you.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current admin setup (or lack of one): where things land, who processes, what rhythm exists
- The renewal inventory as far as known: passports/IDs, insurance policies, vehicle registration and inspection, licenses, key subscriptions, domain names, visas
- Payment plumbing: which bills ride which card/account, and when any card expires
- Past incidents: every late fee, lapse, missed form, or panic-renewal of the last two years
- The bus-factor reality: which accounts, passwords, and processes live in exactly one adult's head
- Any near-term life event that spikes admin load: a move, a birth, a name change, tax season

## Workflow

1. Build the deadline census first: every item in the household that expires or renews, with its date - or "unknown," which is the finding. Documents with multi-year horizons (passports, licenses) are the classic silent failures precisely because no annual rhythm touches them.
2. Hunt the auto-renew and auto-lapse traps in both directions: subscriptions that renew unreviewed (money leak) and policies that *lapse* if a stale card fails (coverage hole - the expensive direction). Cross-check every autopay against its card's expiry date; a card replacement is a mass-lapse event nobody schedules.
3. Score each exposure by escalation curve, not just size: a parking fine triples politely; a tax deadline compounds; an insurance lapse converts a routine premium into an uncovered catastrophe. The items whose cost curves bend hardest get mitigated first.
4. Run the bus-factor test: if the admin-owner adult is hospitalized for a month, can the other adult find the passwords, pay the bills, and locate the policies? If not, that's a top-three risk in any household - mitigation is a shared credential vault and a one-page "how our admin runs" note, not heroic memory.
5. Check the paper trail for disputes: proof-of-payment, policy documents, warranty receipts - are the ones that would matter in a dispute retrievable in five minutes? Unretrievable proof is a risk that only materializes at the worst moment.
6. Close the cheap gaps on the spot: calendar every census date with lead-time alarms (90 days for passports and visas, 30 for policies and registrations, 7 for subscriptions worth reviewing), fix the stale-card autopays, book the shared-vault setup. Each is minutes now against real money later.
7. Set the annual sweep: one dated yearly appointment (birthday month works) to re-run the census, because the census itself goes stale - that's the meta-risk.

## Output format

**Household admin exposure - [date]**

**Deadline census**

| Item | Expires/renews | Known or unknown? | Lead-time alarm set |

**Risk table**

| Exposure | Escalation curve (what it costs at +1 month, +6 months) | Likelihood | Mitigation done/booked |

**Autopay-card cross-check** - bills per card, card expiry dates, mismatches fixed

**Bus-factor findings** - what lives in one head; vault and one-pager status

**Dispute-proof check** - the five-minute retrieval test results

**Fixed today / booked this week** - the closed gaps, listed

**Annual sweep** - the standing date

## Do

- Treat "unknown expiry" as a finding that gets resolved during the review, not noted for later
- Prioritize by escalation curve - the quiet compounding items outrank the loud flat ones
- Set alarms with lead time proportional to bureaucracy (passports get months, not days)
- Write the bus-factor one-pager plainly enough that a stressed person can follow it

## Don't

- Don't audit only the visible pile; the silent renewals nobody has looked at in years are where the review earns its keep
- Don't accept "it's all in my head, it's fine" - that is the risk, restated as reassurance
- Don't let the review produce only awareness; every red item leaves with an alarm, a fix, or a booked action
- Don't store the credential vault's master access where its loss recreates the bus-factor problem
- Don't present penalty amounts or legal consequences as certainties - flag serious tax/legal exposures for professional confirmation
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The census covers documents, policies, registrations, and subscriptions - with unknowns resolved or assigned
- [ ] Every autopay is matched against its card's expiry
- [ ] Risks are ranked by escalation curve with the curve stated
- [ ] The bus-factor test has a concrete answer and a mitigation in motion
- [ ] Every red item ends the review with an alarm, fix, or booked action
- [ ] The annual sweep has a date
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-risk-review or describe the task from the skill description.

# /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-risk-review

Review operational risk for life admin inboxes: a risk table and mitigation plan. Produces a copy-ready risk review. Triggers: "life admin inboxes risk-review", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-risk-review.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Life admin fails silently and bills you later: the passport discovered expired three weeks before the trip, the insurance that lapsed with the changed card number, the tax letter that aged from reminder to penalty in a drawer. Use this to audit a household's admin exposure - where the deadline-bearing items live, which renewals can lapse unnoticed, what breaks if the one admin-competent adult is unavailable - and to close the cheap gaps before one of them invoices you.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The current admin setup (or lack of one): where things land, who processes, what rhythm exists
- The renewal inventory as far as known: passports/IDs, insurance policies, vehicle registration and inspection, licenses, key subscriptions, domain names, visas
- Payment plumbing: which bills ride which card/account, and when any card expires
- Past incidents: every late fee, lapse, missed form, or panic-renewal of the last two years
- The bus-factor reality: which accounts, passwords, and processes live in exactly one adult's head
- Any near-term life event that spikes admin load: a move, a birth, a name change, tax season

## Workflow

1. Build the deadline census first: every item in the household that expires or renews, with its date - or "unknown," which is the finding. Documents with multi-year horizons (passports, licenses) are the classic silent failures precisely because no annual rhythm touches them.
2. Hunt the auto-renew and auto-lapse traps in both directions: subscriptions that renew unreviewed (money leak) and policies that *lapse* if a stale card fails (coverage hole - the expensive direction). Cross-check every autopay against its card's expiry date; a card replacement is a mass-lapse event nobody schedules.
3. Score each exposure by escalation curve, not just size: a parking fine triples politely; a tax deadline compounds; an insurance lapse converts a routine premium into an uncovered catastrophe. The items whose cost curves bend hardest get mitigated first.
4. Run the bus-factor test: if the admin-owner adult is hospitalized for a month, can the other adult find the passwords, pay the bills, and locate the policies? If not, that's a top-three risk in any household - mitigation is a shared credential vault and a one-page "how our admin runs" note, not heroic memory.
5. Check the paper trail for disputes: proof-of-payment, policy documents, warranty receipts - are the ones that would matter in a dispute retrievable in five minutes? Unretrievable proof is a risk that only materializes at the worst moment.
6. Close the cheap gaps on the spot: calendar every census date with lead-time alarms (90 days for passports and visas, 30 for policies and registrations, 7 for subscriptions worth reviewing), fix the stale-card autopays, book the shared-vault setup. Each is minutes now against real money later.
7. Set the annual sweep: one dated yearly appointment (birthday month works) to re-run the census, because the census itself goes stale - that's the meta-risk.

## Output format

**Household admin exposure - [date]**

**Deadline census**

| Item | Expires/renews | Known or unknown? | Lead-time alarm set |

**Risk table**

| Exposure | Escalation curve (what it costs at +1 month, +6 months) | Likelihood | Mitigation done/booked |

**Autopay-card cross-check** - bills per card, card expiry dates, mismatches fixed

**Bus-factor findings** - what lives in one head; vault and one-pager status

**Dispute-proof check** - the five-minute retrieval test results

**Fixed today / booked this week** - the closed gaps, listed

**Annual sweep** - the standing date

## Do

- Treat "unknown expiry" as a finding that gets resolved during the review, not noted for later
- Prioritize by escalation curve - the quiet compounding items outrank the loud flat ones
- Set alarms with lead time proportional to bureaucracy (passports get months, not days)
- Write the bus-factor one-pager plainly enough that a stressed person can follow it

## Don't

- Don't audit only the visible pile; the silent renewals nobody has looked at in years are where the review earns its keep
- Don't accept "it's all in my head, it's fine" - that is the risk, restated as reassurance
- Don't let the review produce only awareness; every red item leaves with an alarm, a fix, or a booked action
- Don't store the credential vault's master access where its loss recreates the bus-factor problem
- Don't present penalty amounts or legal consequences as certainties - flag serious tax/legal exposures for professional confirmation
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The census covers documents, policies, registrations, and subscriptions - with unknowns resolved or assigned
- [ ] Every autopay is matched against its card's expiry
- [ ] Risks are ranked by escalation curve with the curve stated
- [ ] The bus-factor test has a concrete answer and a mitigation in motion
- [ ] Every red item ends the review with an alarm, fix, or booked action
- [ ] The annual sweep has a date
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate

# /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-stakeholder-brief

Prepare stakeholder communication for life admin inboxes: a brief stakeholders can understand quickly. Produces a copy-ready stakeholder brief. Triggers: "life admin inboxes stakeholder-brief", /personal-productivity-life-life-admin-inbox-stakeholder-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A new admin system only holds if everyone who generates or handles paperwork plays along: the partner who must stop leaving mail in the car, the teenager whose school forms need to reach the tray, the accountant who needs documents by a date, the ex-partner who still receives the kid's medical letters. And the sorting itself surfaces items that need other people's action - a form only the partner can sign, a subscription in the other adult's name. Use this to brief the household on how admin now works, and to route the surfaced items to their owners with deadlines.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The new system's shape: where the inbox is, who owns processing, when the weekly block runs
- Everyone who generates admin into the household: adults, kids, school, the accountant, an ex, aging parents whose affairs the user helps manage
- The action items surfaced from the backlog that belong to someone else - with their real deadlines
- What the other adults must be able to do in the owner's absence (the deputy scope)
- Any sensitivities: finances not shared with kids, matters not shared with the ex

## Workflow

1. Write the house rule announcement as three sentences, not a policy document: where admin lands (the tray, always), when it gets processed (the weekly block), and the one ask of everyone ("if it's paper and it matters, it goes in the tray - never the counter, the car, or your bag"). One rule that everyone follows beats five that only the author remembers.
2. Route the surfaced items person by person, each as a single message: the item, why it's theirs, the concrete action, the deadline, and what happens if it slips ("the form needs your signature by Thursday or she misses the trip"). Group multiple items per person into one list - five separate nags read as five nags.
3. Brief the deputy properly: where the vault/passwords note lives, which bills are autopay versus manual, what the weekly block covers, and the short list of "if I'm unreachable, these three things cannot slip." This is a 15-minute walkthrough plus a one-pager, not an email.
4. Give external professionals (accountant, lawyer, property manager) the packet format they actually want: what documents, by what date, in what form (originals vs scans), and the single question list - batched, so the relationship stays efficient and billable hours stay low.
5. Handle the delicate channels deliberately: the ex-partner note sticks to logistics ("school letters now come to me; I'll forward medical ones within a day"); the aging-parent conversation asks permission before taking over anything ("would it help if I tracked the renewal dates?"). Tone here is the difference between coordination and offense.
6. Set the household feedback loop: at the one-month mark, one question at dinner - "what's annoying about the tray system?" - and adjust. A system people were consulted on gets maintained; one that was decreed gets quietly sabotaged.

## Output format

**Admin system comms - [household]**

**House rule announcement** - the three sentences, ready to say or post by the tray

**Routed items** (one message per person, ready to send)
- **[Name]** - their items as a checklist: action · deadline · consequence if slipped

**Deputy brief** - the one-pager: vault location, autopay vs manual map, the three things that can't slip, weekly block scope

**Professional packets** - per pro: documents, dates, format, batched questions

**Delicate notes** - the ex/parent messages, logistics-only tone, ready to adapt

**One-month check** - the date and the single question

## Do

- Keep the house rule small enough to say in one breath - compliance tracks simplicity
- Batch each person's items into one message with deadlines and consequences
- Walk the deputy through the system live; a document nobody has been shown is a document that doesn't exist in a crisis
- Ask permission before absorbing anyone else's admin, especially a parent's

## Don't

- Don't send the household a process manifesto - enthusiasm for the new system is the fastest way to get it ignored
- Don't include financial details in messages to kids, or anything beyond logistics in messages to an ex
- Don't let routed items go out without deadlines; an undated request is a future pile item
- Don't share vault access or account credentials over insecure channels - the deputy brief points to where credentials live, it never contains them
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't send anything on the user's behalf without explicit approval - messages to family, exes, and professionals are a sensitive action

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The house rule fits in three sentences and one location
- [ ] Every routed item has an owner, action, deadline, and consequence
- [ ] The deputy brief would let a stressed adult run one month of admin cold
- [ ] Delicate notes contain logistics only, in a tone that preserves the relationship
- [ ] No credentials travel in any message; nothing sends without user approval
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```
